threats.title: Arbitrary Caller Can Steal NFT During Isolate Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: The executeIsolateLiquidate function transfers the liquidated NFT to the caller (params.msgSender) instead of the winning bidder (loanData.lastBidder), allowing anyone to steal the NFT using the winning bidder's escrowed funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function lacks access control to ensure the caller is the winning bidder. It uses the escrowed bidAmount to repay the loan but transfers the underlying collateral to msgSender.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc721TransferIsolateSupplyOnLiquidate(nftAssetData, params.msgSender, params.nftTokenIds) uses params.msgSender instead of loanData.lastBidder

preconditions: An isolate loan auction has ended (block.timestamp > auctionEndTimestamp)
A valid bid was placed during the auction

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Transferring asset to msgSender while using another user's stored funds
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The NFT is awarded to the transaction sender, but the debt is cleared using loanData.bidAmount which belongs to loanData.lastBidder.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing require(params.msgSender == loanData.lastBidder) or transferring to lastBidder directly.

analysis_questions: Can an attacker front-run the legitimate liquidator to claim the NFT?
Are there any hidden checks in ValidateLogic that prevent this?
validation_requirements: Create an isolate loan and trigger an auction
Place a bid from Account A
Call isolateLiquidate from Account B and verify Account B receives the NFT

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker monitors the blockchain for isolate auctions that have just ended. They call isolateLiquidate. The protocol uses the legitimate highest bidder's funds to clear the debt and transfers the NFT to the attacker for free.
prerequisites: Ended isolate auction with a bid

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of NFT collateral for the winning bidder, who loses their bid funds but does not receive the NFT.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Unrecoverable once the NFT is transferred to the attacker.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test verifying that only the winning bidder receives the NFT upon liquidation.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable on-chain as the NFT transfer recipient differs from the lastBidder.

threats.title: Liquidated NFTs Become Permanently Locked Due to Uncleared lockerAddr
threats.hypothesis_description: When an isolate loan is liquidated, the protocol fails to clear the tokenData.lockerAddr. The NFT becomes permanently locked and cannot be withdrawn by the liquidator or any future depositor.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lockerAddr is set to address(this) upon borrowing to prevent withdrawal. During liquidation, the loan is deleted and ownership is transferred, but lockerAddr is never reset to address(0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: State updates clear owner and supplyMode but omit lockerAddr

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Incomplete state cleanup during asset transfer/burn

analysis_questions: Does any other function clear the lockerAddr post-liquidation?
Can the liquidator withdraw the NFT if supplyAsCollateral is true?
validation_requirements: Liquidate an isolate loan with supplyAsCollateral = true
Attempt to withdraw the NFT as the liquidator
Verify the transaction reverts with ASSET_ALREADY_LOCKED_IN_USE

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A liquidator successfully wins an auction and calls isolateLiquidate with supplyAsCollateral = true. The NFT is credited to their supply. When they attempt to call withdrawERC721, the transaction reverts because lockerAddr is still set, permanently trapping the NFT in the protocol.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of access to liquidated NFTs for liquidators or future depositors.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to clear the stuck state.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing end-to-end test for withdrawing an NFT after it has been acquired via liquidation.

threats.title: All Native ETH Payable Functions Revert Due to Incorrect WETH transferFrom Usage
threats.hypothesis_description: Functions accepting native ETH (msg.value) wrap it to WETH and send it to the user, but subsequent logic attempts to pull the WETH back using transferFrom without prior approval, causing guaranteed reverts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The wrapNativeTokenInWallet function transfers minted WETH to the user. Immediately after, functions like erc20TransferInLiquidity call safeTransferFrom(user, address(this), amount). Since the user is an EOA sending ETH, they have not approved the protocol to spend their WETH.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IWETH.transferFrom(address(this), user, amount) followed by IERC20.safeTransferFrom(user, address(this), amount)

preconditions: User interacts with a payable function using native ETH (msg.value > 0)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Sending tokens to a user and immediately pulling them back without allowance
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The protocol assumes it can pull WETH from the user just because the user sent ETH, but ERC20 allowance rules strictly prevent this.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Protocol should keep the WETH internally rather than sending it to the user before pulling it.

analysis_questions: Does the WETH implementation bypass allowance checks if the sender is the protocol? (No, it only bypasses if src == msg.sender)
Are there any paths where the user naturally has infinite WETH allowance set?
validation_requirements: Call depositERC20 with native ETH from an account with 0 WETH allowance
Verify the transaction reverts with an ERC20 insufficient allowance error

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A normal user attempts to deposit ETH into the protocol by calling depositERC20 with msg.value. The transaction reverts, rendering the native ETH integration completely unusable.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Core protocol functionality (native ETH deposits/repayments) is broken.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting to use native ETH.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users must manually wrap ETH to WETH and use the ERC20 flow.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests using actual EOAs without pre-existing WETH approvals.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: High rate of reverted transactions interacting with payable functions.

threats.title: Isolate Redeem Decreases Caller's Debt Instead of Borrower's, Creating Unrepayable Phantom Debt
threats.hypothesis_description: The executeIsolateRedeem function incorrectly decreases the isolate borrow balance of the caller (params.msgSender) instead of the actual NFT owner. This allows an attacker to reduce their own debt while leaving the victim with phantom debt.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function uses params.msgSender in erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow. If a third party redeems the NFT, the third party's debt is reduced, but the original borrower's userScaledIsolateBorrow remains inflated even though the loan is cleared.

proof_of_signal.pattern: VaultLogic.erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow(debtGroupData, params.msgSender, vars.amountScaled) uses msgSender instead of tokenData.owner

preconditions: Victim has an isolate loan in AUCTION status
Attacker has an active isolate borrow in the same debt group

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Modifying state for msgSender instead of the authenticated asset owner
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The accounting between aggregate user debt and individual loan debt becomes permanently desynchronized.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing check that params.msgSender == tokenData.owner, or passing tokenData.owner to the decrease function.

analysis_questions: Can the victim ever clear their phantom debt?
Does the phantom debt accrue interest and cause cross-margin liquidations?
validation_requirements: Create an isolate loan for User A and trigger auction
Create an isolate loan for User B
User B calls isolateRedeem for User A's NFT
Verify User B's debt decreases and User A's debt remains unchanged

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker B sees Victim A's NFT in auction. Attacker B calls isolateRedeem. Attacker B pays the redeem amount, and their own isolate debt is reduced by that amount. Victim A gets their NFT back, but their userScaledIsolateBorrow is never reduced. Victim A now has phantom debt that lowers their health factor and cannot be repaid because they have no active loans.
prerequisites: Attacker must have existing debt to absorb the reduction

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victims suffer permanent account bricking and potential cross-margin liquidations due to artificially inflated debt.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Borrowers whose NFTs go into auction.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade to fix accounting.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests where a third party redeems an isolate loan.

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient funds to pay the redeem amount
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Mismatch between sum of loanData.scaledAmount and userScaledIsolateBorrow.

threats.title: Native ETH Withdrawals Revert When Requested Amount Exceeds Balance
threats.hypothesis_description: When withdrawing native ETH, if the requested amount exceeds the user's balance (e.g., using type(uint256).max to withdraw all), the internal logic caps the withdrawal but the unwrap logic attempts to pull the original uncapped amount, causing a revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: SupplyLogic.executeWithdrawERC20 caps params.amount to userBalance. However, BVault.withdrawERC20 passes the original 'amount' variable to unwrapNativeTokenInWallet, which tries to withdraw more WETH than the user actually received.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Pass-by-value 'amount' is used in unwrapNativeTokenInWallet after being potentially modified inside executeWithdrawERC20's struct

preconditions: User calls withdrawERC20 with asset = NATIVE_TOKEN_ADDRESS
Requested amount > user's actual supply balance

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Using stale variables after state-modifying calls that adjust limits
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The unwrap function will attempt to pull type(uint256).max WETH from the user, which will fail.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: executeWithdrawERC20 should return the actual withdrawn amount to be used by the unwrap function.

analysis_questions: Does this completely break frontend integrations that use max uint256 for 'withdraw all'?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user clicks 'Withdraw All' on the frontend, which sends type(uint256).max as the amount. The transaction reverts, preventing them from withdrawing their ETH.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Users are unable to use standard 'withdraw all' functionality for native ETH.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Users must manually query their exact balance and input it precisely.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for withdrawing native ETH with amount > balance.

threats.title: Locked NFTs Can Be Flash Loaned, Breaking Yield and Isolate Loan Invariants
threats.hypothesis_description: The executeFlashLoanERC721 function does not check if an NFT is locked (tokenData.lockerAddr != address(0)). This allows users to flash loan NFTs that are actively staked in external yield protocols or used as isolate collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the function checks that the caller is the owner and that the isolate loan status is ACTIVE, it completely ignores the lockerAddr. If an NFT is locked by a yield manager, transferring it out via flash loan breaks the assumption that the NFT remains in the pool's custody.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing require(tokenData.lockerAddr == address(0)) check before transferring NFT

preconditions: An NFT is deposited and locked (e.g., staked in Lido/EtherFi via Yield module)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Bypassing lock mechanisms during temporary transfers
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: External yield protocols may be manipulated or desynchronized if the staked NFT is moved out of the pool during a flash loan callback.

analysis_questions: Can the flash loan receiver call external yield contracts to unstake or claim rewards directly?
Does moving the NFT break internal accounting in the Yield module?
validation_requirements: Stake an NFT in the Yield module
In the callback, attempt to interact with the external yield protocol (e.g., Lido) using the NFT

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user stakes their NFT to earn yield. They then flash loan the NFT to themselves. During the callback, they use the NFT to claim airdrops or interact with the yield protocol in ways that should be restricted while staked, extracting unauthorized value.
prerequisites: NFT must be locked by a yield manager

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of yield, airdrops, or desynchronization of external protocol state.

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests combining Yield staking with Flash Loans.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Flash loan events for NFTs that have non-zero lockerAddr.
